draft.introduction: The Mahāyāna Nirvana Sutra proclaims that all beings possess Buddha-nature (tathāgatagarbha). Even those with no apparent spiritual inclination will eventually attain awakening.

translation.text: The Buddha said: “All beings possess the Tathāgata’s wisdom and virtue. It is only because of deluded thinking and attachment that they fail to realize it. If they would abandon delusion, then omniscience and natural wisdom would manifest.”
translation.original_text: sarva-sattvāḥ tathāgata-garbhaḥ | kevalaṃ kleśāvaraṇāt na prajñāyante

translation.text: The Self of the Tathāgata is permanent, blissful, and pure. Those who say the Buddha taught that there is no Self have not understood the complete meaning. In the ultimate sense, the Tathāgata-garbha is the true Self.

translator_notes.note: The assertion of a true “Self” (ātman) shocked Buddhist scholastics committed to no-self (anātman). The text resolves this by distinguishing the conventional no-self teaching (a skillful means for beginners) from the ultimate teaching of Buddha-nature as the deathless, unconditioned essence.
